one_line_takeaway: Kraken is the first physical EM side-channel attack on GPU Tensor Cores, using a warp-level leakage model and novel higher-order DNN attacks to extract CNN weights far more efficiently than prior CUDA-Core work, and gives the first (proof-of-concept) demonstration that LLM weight leakage is observable in the far field, even through glass at 100 cm.
key_finding: Tensor Core computations leak extractable weight information, and the warp-level model recovers all 288 weights of the CNN's first layer with ~100k traces on average (vs. millions for BarraCUDA), despite Kraken running at a higher 920 MHz clock; the higher-order attack combining 3 warp-level intermediates drives the correct-key rank to 0 after only ~10,000 traces. In the far field, weight- and input-dependent leakage of an LLM is observable at 25 cm and even at 100 cm through glass, and a PoC recovers the 8th weight of Wq/Wk/Wv to low key rank from 100 cm through glass, establishing that unfettered near-field access is not strictly required.
core_contribution: Kraken presents EM side-channel weight-extraction attacks against DNNs running on GPU Tensor Cores, which prior physical-SCA work had never targeted (earlier attacks, notably BarraCUDA, hit only general-purpose CUDA Cores). Three distinct own-contributions: (1) a GPU-specific 'warp-level' leakage model that sums the modeled power of all 32 threads in a warp that operate on the same weight, giving a far more accurate energy model than the single-thread model of prior work; (2) the first 'higher-order' side-channel attack on DNNs, a platform-agnostic technique that combines leakage from multiple time samples (multiple IMMA/HMMA instructions) that all depend on the same weight, analogous to higher-order attacks used against masking in cryptographic SCA; and (3) the first proof-of-concept far-field EM side-channel analysis of an LLM (Llama 3.2 1B on an RTX 4090), showing weight- and input-related leakage observable up to 100 cm away and even through a glass obstacle. The paper also contributes a CPA complexity analysis that reduces per-weight extraction of bfloat16 LLM weights from a naive 128-bit search to at most 44 bits, and a discussion of how weight quantization itself leaks information before any measurement.
threat_model: An adversary wanting to steal a proprietary/expensively-trained model's weights (IP) without API access, using physical EM emanations instead of query-based extraction (thereby bypassing rate-limiting and output obfuscation). Near-field variant: attacker has physical access to the GPU die (Jetson Orin Nano, flip-chip package with exposed silicon) and can place an EM probe over a Streaming Multiprocessor sub-partition; can apply chosen/zeroed inputs to a CNN. Far-field variant (explicitly a WEAKER adversary than all prior work): attacker only needs a directional antenna and SDR at 25-100 cm, no heatsink removal or die contact, and targets an open-source base LLM fine-tuned with LoRA (so only a small subset of weights must be recovered) whose architecture is assumed already identified. For LLMs the embedding layer prevents chosen-input attacks, so the attacker must instead fix the 32-bit 's2' accumulator state to isolate a single weight.
limitations: The far-field attack is explicitly a proof-of-concept: leakage is observable but full weight extraction from far field is 'not yet practical' and glass reduces leakage. Because LLMs use an embedding layer, chosen-input (zeroing) attacks do not work on LLMs, forcing the attacker toward harder techniques (e.g., attacking individual Tensor Core multiplications, potentially laser fault injection) to fix the s1 state. Full-model extraction (all layers) remains infeasible mainly due to the compute needed to enumerate every weight; the paper only recovers small subsets, which it argues suffices for the LoRA scenario. The near-field CNN attack still needs chosen inputs to be feasible. Partial sums inside the Tensor Cores themselves are not attacked (their exact computation is unclear), and multi-target CPA is left as future work. Even the reduced per-weight complexity is 'at most 44 bits' and must be repeated per weight.

datasets_used: 2-layer CNN with 32 kernels/layer, 3x3 INT8 weights (near-field target on Jetson Orin Nano; first layer has 288 weights)
Llama 3.2 1B (bfloat16 weights, HuggingFace transformers 4.51.3; far-field target on RTX 4090)
quantitative_results: Warp-level model: ~100k traces on average extract weights (all 288 first-layer weights recovered), vs. millions for BarraCUDA; BarraCUDA-style approach reproduced needs ~300k on average in this setup
Higher-order attack combining 3 warp-level intermediates: correct-key rank reaches 0 after ~10,000 traces
Near-field clock 920 MHz (vs. 625 MHz in BarraCUDA); oscilloscope sampling 20 GS/s

IMMA.16816.S8.S8 result can depend on up to 16 weights (16x8 = 128-bit complexity); implementation loads only 4 weights/kernel; PoC recovers 8 bits (one weight) at a time by zeroing 3 of 4 inputs; first layer executed by 512 threads = 16 warps across 4 SMs
Far-field: RTX 4090 core-clock boost 2565 MHz center frequency, SDR bandwidth 2 MHz / sample rate 4 MHz; batch 128 raises clock to 2865 MHz but 2565 MHz still leaks
Far-field near/far boundary R = 2D^2/lambda with lambda ~= 12 cm, D = 2.5 cm gives R ~= 1.04 cm (~1 cm)
LLM CPA complexity reduced from naive 128 bits to at most 44 bits per weight; weight range 1e-10 < |w| < 1 in Llama first layer yields 5106 candidate bfloat16 values (~12.318 bits)

Far-field PoC extraction (8th weight of Wq/Wk/Wv, first 7 assumed known, 5106 candidates): 500 traces averaged per input x 4000 inputs = 2,000,000 traces at 100 cm through glass; 1M traces at 25 cm without glass already beats 2M traces at 100 cm through glass
TVLA far-field weight leakage plots use 300,000 traces (batch 32 and batch 128); input-leakage TVLA uses 50,000 traces (batch 32)
baselines_compared: BarraCUDA [27] - prior GPU weight-extraction attack on general-purpose CUDA Cores at 625 MHz requiring millions of traces (Kraken's main quantitative baseline)
CSI-NN / Batina et al. [9] - seminal DPA-based DNN architecture reverse-engineering via EM (cited as origin of the attack class, not a numeric baseline)
Single-thread leakage model of prior work (contrasted against Kraken's warp-level all-32-thread model)
